summary: Spolsky argues that platform companies must recognize they are platform companies and prioritize developer adoption above all else. He examines historical examples of platform failures (NetWare, Apple, OS/2, IBM PS/2) where vendors either didn't realize they had a platform or got greedy and locked out developers. He contrasts these with successes like Windows and Java, which offered free or cheap runtimes and development tools. The bulk of the post uses Groove as a case study — a technology with compelling P2P architecture that is doomed because it charges for its runtime and treats itself as an application rather than a platform. Spolsky concludes that platforms need free runtimes to achieve the massive developer adoption required for success.
key_points: Platforms must be marketed to developers first, not end users — failing to recognize this distinction is a primary cause of platform failure
Greedy platform companies that try to capture all revenue themselves choke off the developer ecosystem that would make them successful
A free redistributable runtime is essentially mandatory for platform adoption — no developer will build on a platform that adds cost to their product
Groove has compelling P2P technology but is positioning itself as an application rather than a platform, ensuring it will be displaced by a free or open-source alternative
Windows succeeded by never making developers pay for the runtime, starting with a free redistributable and eventually being bundled with every PC
money_quotes: It's really, really important to figure out if your product is a platform or not, because platforms need to be marketed in a very different way to be successful.
The best way to kill a platform is to make it hard for developers to build on it.
Greedy platform companies can't stand the idea of all kinds of unwashed riffraff making money off of their platform, so they make it darn near impossible for anyone to develop for it.
As a platform vendor, you're only as successful as the people who build on you.
Anybody who ever tried to sell software components knows that you have to have a royalty-free runtime or no developer will touch you with a 10 foot pole.
If Notes had a software industry sitting on top of it way back in the 80s, some dormroom startup might have made a compelling hypertext application for it instead and preempted the Web.
Someone else will come along with a P2P architecture and sell it like a component, or make it an open source library, and that's what software developers will use.
key_insight: A platform's success is entirely determined by developer adoption, which requires free runtimes and low barriers to entry — platform vendors who get greedy or mistake their platform for an application are signing their own death warrant.
